<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 4: Choosing the right question to answer

Your group will choose one research question to guide the rest of your work in the session.

* Step 1: Propose four candidate research questions.

* Step 2: Score each question from 0–2 on each criterion below (0 = weak, 1 = okay, 2 = strong).
  - Useful: Would answering it help Uber decide what to do?
  - Answerable: Could you imagine what you would measure to answer it?
  - Specific: Is it narrow enough to be answered in a short investigation?

* Step 3: Choose the highest-scoring question and rewrite it to be as clear as possible.


<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective** 
Students should:

Evaluate questions using three practical criteria (useful, answerable, specific).

Rewrite a chosen question into a clearer, more decision-relevant form.

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

Research question = the engine of the whole analysis: it determines what data you need and what you can conclude.

Useful (decision relevance): a good question changes what Uber would do next week (monitor, redesign, enforce, communicate).

Answerable (measurable): if you can’t name an outcome you’d measure, it’s not an analytics question yet.

Specific (scope): narrow beats broad—time window, city/area, and a concrete behavior/outcome.

Rewrite skill: most first drafts are vague; improvement comes from tightening nouns (what outcome? what behavior? where/when?).

💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

“What part of this question is fuzzy—behavior, outcome, or scope?”

“Rewrite it so a manager could read it once and know what you mean.”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

Writing goals instead of questions (“understand what’s happening,” “explore driver behavior”).

Vague verbs with no measurement (“impact,” “influence,” “affect”) without naming what would be measured.

Questions that are too broad (no city/time/area; multiple outcomes; multiple mechanisms).

Scoring “useful” based on what sounds interesting rather than what informs a decision.

Treating “answerable” as “easy” (it’s about measurability, not difficulty).

Rewriting only for wording, not substance (clarity improves when you add outcome/behavior/scope, not just synonyms).

:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

No solution provided. Depends on what the group comes up with.
:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
